title: Open Problem: Separating Geometric and Algorithmic Compression via Cayley-Table Completion
abstract: Modern statistical learning theory and deep learning characterize generalization primarily in terms of continuous capacity control (e.g., norm-based regularization, margin maximization, low-rank bias). While highly successful in continuous domains, deep learning consistently fails to extrapolate exact algorithmic or discrete algebraic rules, reflecting a missing inductive bias toward algorithmic complexity minimization. We propose the Cayley-table completion as the canonical testbed for this missing bias, serving as the discrete algebraic counterpart to matrix completion. Just as matrix factorization combined with weight decay yields an implicit geometric bias toward low linear rank, recent results demonstrate that operator-valued tensor factorizations paired with a flatness prior yield an implicit algorithmic bias toward exact discrete associativity. We pose the open problem of establishing formal exact recovery bounds for Cayley-table completion, and challenge the community to generalize continuous flatness priors to autonomously discover broader discrete algorithmic axioms without combinatorial search.
summary: This work tackles the fundamental theoretical challenge of moving deep learning beyond continuous geometric assumptions to robustly discover discrete, exact algebraic laws. By framing the problem as Cayley-table completion, the authors propose using specialized tensor factorization techniques to test for underlying discrete axioms like associativity. This research aims to provide the mathematical tools necessary for AI to autonomously derive fundamental physical laws.
main_problem: The paper addresses the theoretical gap between modern deep learning's reliance on continuous geometric compression and the need to model discrete, exact algorithmic rules.
main_result: It proposes Cayley-table completion as a canonical testbed to formally establish recovery bounds for discrete algebraic structures, bridging continuous and discrete learning theory.
method: The authors use operator-valued tensor factorizations regularized by a flatness prior to implicitly encode and test for discrete algebraic axioms, contrasting this with standard low-rank geometric regularization.
model_or_system: The primary model is Cayley-Table Completion, which involves recovering a discrete binary operation (a group structure) from partial observations, serving as the discrete analogue to matrix completion.
key_observables: Exact discrete associativity, low linear rank (geometric bias), and the formal recovery bounds for the completion problem.
important_parameters: The regularization strength of the flatness prior ($\mathcal{H}(\Theta)$) and the sample size ($O(n \log n)$ entries).
main_assumptions: The core assumption is that algorithmic complexity minimization can be formalized and tested using continuous optimization techniques like flatness priors.
paper_structure: The paper is structured around identifying the theoretical divide between geometric and algorithmic compression, proposing Cayley-table completion as the testbed, detailing the tensor factorization approach, and concluding with open problems regarding formal separation theorems.
why_interesting: While highly theoretical, the focus on finding rigorous inductive biases for machine learning has implications for modeling complex, discrete physical systems where simple continuous approximations fail.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
